title: AI Export Controls in Flux: Anthropic Freed, DeepSeek Held, Allies Scramble
subhead: The US lifted export controls on Anthropic's frontier models after an 18-day standoff, while holding off on blacklisting over 100 Chinese firms including DeepSeek. Meanwhile, India-Japan forged a new economic security pact and Kioxia shipped next-gen memory — the AI supply chain is being redrawn in real time.

sections.heading: Anthropic's 18-Day Export Control Ordeal Ends — But the Precedent Is Set
sections.body:
On June 30, the US Commerce Department lifted export controls on Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, ending an 18-day suspension that had disabled the models globally for all users. The saga began June 12, when Commerce ordered Anthropic to block all foreign national access to the two models, citing a potential jailbreak vulnerability. Anthropic, unable to filter by nationality in real time on shared cloud infrastructure, shut both models down worldwide — taking offline systems used by enterprise clients across finance, healthcare, and critical infrastructure on AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry.

White House adviser David Sacks had claimed Anthropic declined to fix the issue; Anthropic disputed this, arguing the finding involved a narrow potential vulnerability and that applying the same standard industry-wide would halt all frontier model deployment. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick initially eased restrictions only partially — permitting Mythos 5 access for select companies and federal agencies — before the full reversal on June 30. A coalition of information security leaders published an open letter calling for the controls to be lifted, arguing AI regulation should be grounded in scientific evaluation and democratic rule-making. The pressure, alongside criticism that the ban handed competitive ground to Chinese open-source developers, shaped the outcome, according to Forbes.

The episode established two precedents: first, that the US government can and will use export-control authority over AI models themselves, not just the chips that run them; second, that a unilateral kill-switch on frontier models can disable critical enterprise infrastructure globally within hours. The G7 'trusted partners' discussion (see below) was a direct reaction to this event.
sections.whyItMatters: For the first time, the US exercised export-control authority over specific AI models rather than hardware — and the two-week global shutdown demonstrated how fragile model access can be when policy decisions override commercial availability. Every enterprise relying on frontier AI now knows a single government directive can turn off their most capable systems overnight.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms — Diplomacy Over Security
sections.body:
An interagency committee approved more than 100 Chinese companies — including AI startup DeepSeek and memory chipmaker CXMT — for addition to the Commerce Department's Entity List last year, but the Trump administration has held off publishing the listings, wary of escalating tensions with Beijing, Reuters first reported. At least 75 of those entities are in advanced semiconductor production, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and AI modeling, according to CNBC.

The security case is well-documented: a senior State Department official told Reuters that DeepSeek has supported China's military and intelligence operations and attempted to use Southeast Asian shell companies to illegally access advanced US chips. Anthropic identified a campaign by DeepSeek and two other Chinese labs to extract capabilities from its Claude platform, and OpenAI warned lawmakers that DeepSeek was targeting its models too. CXMT, China's top memory chipmaker, was designated a Chinese military company by the Pentagon under the Biden administration.

Despite all this, Commerce Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler has sought to avoid listing Chinese parties since late 2025, according to Reuters sources. The Entity List has had no new additions since October — the longest gap in over a decade, per CSIS analyst Philip Luck. Kevin Kurland, a former Commerce official, said the hold demonstrates that 'trade policy is overshadowing the use of a critical national security tool.' Multiple Chinese companies identified for supplying Russian drones recovered in Poland, and dozens flagged for selling restricted Nvidia chips to Chinese universities, also remain unlisted.

China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian called on the US to 'stop politicizing, instrumentalizing and weaponizing trade and tech issues,' while Huaqiao University professor Hu Qimu told the Global Times the hold was 'a positive signal to help further stabilize bilateral ties.' The gap between the interagency security verdict and the political decision not to act is the story: the firms Washington most wants to constrain are precisely the ones Beijing would treat as most provocative.
sections.whyItMatters: The US has a fully vetted blacklist of 100+ Chinese companies ready to publish and is choosing not to — prioritizing diplomatic de-escalation over the security agencies' recommendations. Meanwhile, those firms continue operating with access to US technology and markets, and the Entity List freeze means American suppliers have no visibility into which counterparties are flagged risks.

sections.heading: G7 Debates 'Trusted Partners' AI Access After Anthropic Shutdown Shocks Allies
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, multiple member countries raised with US Commerce Secretary Lutnick the idea of a 'trusted partners' scheme that would let allied nations or companies access the most advanced US AI models, Reuters reported. The proposal was a direct reaction to Anthropic's June 12 global shutdown of Mythos 5 and Fable 5 — which took the models offline for everyone, including G7 allies, because selective filtering by nationality wasn't technically feasible on shared cloud services.

The discussions, which took place over a dinner on the summit's opening night, remain exploratory. 'Trusted partners' could be countries or individual companies, the sources said. French President Emmanuel Macron said he expected progress on broadening access to Anthropic's models 'in coming weeks,' according to Reuters. The argument pitched to Washington is defensive: broader access to frontier models would let G7 countries build stronger cyber-defences against rivals, principally China — appealing in Washington's own security language.

What the episode made clear is that America's closest allies now recognize that the most advanced AI they can buy is American, access to it is a lever in Washington's hand, and the only available response is to ask, politely, for it back. The G7 leaders also pledged closer coordination on AI risks generally, but no formal agreement on model access was reached.
sections.whyItMatters: AI model access is now a diplomatic issue at the highest level. The Anthropic shutdown demonstrated that the US can unilaterally cut off its closest allies from frontier models, and the 'trusted partners' concept — still just an idea over dinner — is the first structured attempt to create a lane for allied access. Whether Washington concedes ground here will shape how European and Asian capitals think about AI sovereignty and domestic model development.

sections.heading: India-Japan Economic Security Pact Targets Semiconductors, Critical Minerals, and AI
sections.body:
Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi completed a three-day visit to India this week, producing 16 agreements and a landmark Joint Declaration on Economic Security Cooperation covering five strategic sectors: semiconductors, critical minerals, information and communications, clean energy, and pharmaceuticals. The two countries also agreed on their first-ever defence co-development project — a naval communications system under the UNICORN next-generation programme — and committed to holding a 2+2 ministerial meeting this year.

The declaration explicitly references 'economic coercion and non-market policies and practices, including arbitrary export restrictions that may lead to supply chain disruptions, particularly on critical minerals,' a clear reference to China's dominance of rare earth supply chains and recent export restrictions on Japan. India's Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal stated publicly that the semiconductor supply chain is 'too concentrated in China.' Japan has faced direct Chinese pressure since Takaichi said a Chinese attack on Taiwan could pose an 'existential threat' to Japan — Beijing responded with rare earth export restrictions, detention of Japanese businessmen, and bans on Japanese seafood.

China's Foreign Ministry warned that cooperation between India and Japan 'should not target or harm the interests of third parties, let alone serve as a pretext for forming exclusive cliques or stoking confrontation.' Both countries' largest trading partner remains China, making the partnership simultaneously a commercial necessity and a source of strategic discomfort.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant India-Japan economic security agreement to date, moving the relationship from economic convenience toward a quasi-alliance of supply chain diversification. Both countries are explicitly targeting China's leverage over critical minerals and semiconductors — but with China as their biggest trade partner, the practical limits of decoupling are real.

sections.heading: CXMT Lands $3B Tencent Deal and IPO Approval as China's Memory Stack Consolidates
sections.body:
ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) — the same chipmaker the US has held off blacklisting — signed a long-term supply agreement with Tencent worth over 20 billion yuan (~$3 billion), according to Reuters and DigiTimes. The deal positions CXMT as a major domestic supplier of DRAM to China's largest cloud and gaming platform, which runs AI workloads through Tencent Cloud and backs companies including Moonshot AI.

Simultaneously, China's securities regulator approved CXMT's IPO registration for a listing on Shanghai's STAR Market, China Daily reported, giving the company fresh capital to expand production and upgrade its technology. SemiAnalysis estimates cited by Barron's project CXMT could grow its share of the global DRAM market from 11% in 2025 to 17% by 2028 — though global DRAM supply may remain tight as AI demand outpaces even expanded capacity.

The timing is notable: CXMT is simultaneously building market share with China's largest tech companies, going public, and facing the prospect of a US Entity List listing that the security bureaucracy has approved but the political leadership has shelved. If CXMT continues to scale with domestic customers like Tencent, the practical impact of any future US blacklist diminishes — Chinese cloud platforms would already have a home-grown alternative.
sections.whyItMatters: CXMT's $3 billion Tencent deal shows how China's AI supply chain is consolidating around domestic alternatives. Each major contract that bypasses Samsung, SK Hynix, or Micron reduces the leverage of future US export controls. The company is simultaneously being groomed as China's national memory champion and sitting on a US blacklist that Washington won't publish — a gap that narrows the longer it stays open.

sections.heading: Kioxia Ships Next-Gen Memory, SK Hynix Bets $60B+ on NAND — the AI Memory Race Accelerates
sections.body:
Kioxia held a ceremony at its fab in Kitakami, Iwate Prefecture, on July 3, shipping samples of its 10th-generation BiCS Flash memory developed with Sandisk. CEO Hiroo Ota said the company will 'expand production at Fab2, which is furnished with state-of-the-art equipment, to fully meet growing market demand.' Kioxia's stock has surged more than sevenfold this year, pushing its market capitalisation past $250 billion — exceeding Toyota Motor's — as AI inference drives demand for high-capacity NAND. Analyst Kazuyoshi Saito of IwaiCosmo Securities estimates Kioxia is two to four years ahead of rivals in NAND performance and power consumption, aided by its wafer bonding technology.

The NAND boom is spurring massive new investment: SK Hynix announced it will spend 80 trillion won (~$52 billion) on a new NAND fab, plus an additional 13 trillion won on a chip packaging plant — over $60 billion total. SK Hynix, a pioneer of high-bandwidth memory for AI training, is now pivoting hard toward NAND as inference demand grows.

Japan's government is backing the sector: Prime Minister Takaichi's government in March set a goal of boosting domestically produced chip sales fivefold by 2040, and a Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry official said Kioxia's competitiveness is 'very important from the perspective of the national interest and securing stable supply chains for allies and like-minded countries.' Japan controlled half the global chip market in the 1980s but has since fallen below 10%.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI memory market is bifurcating: DRAM for training, NAND for inference. Kioxia's comeback from a struggling Toshiba spinoff to Japan's most valuable company shows how fast the NAND demand curve has shifted. SK Hynix's $60B+ bet confirms that the biggest memory players see NAND as the next bottleneck — and both Japan and Korea are treating memory production as a national security priority.

sections.heading: DHS Cyber Breach Exposes Sensitive Information-Sharing Network Ahead of World Cup
sections.body:
The US Department of Homeland Security confirmed it is investigating a breach of the Homeland Security Information Network (HSIN), a sensitive but unclassified platform used by federal, state, local, territorial, tribal, international, and private-sector partners to share mission-critical information. Hackers accessed HSIN servers and a SharePoint system sometime between late May and early June, according to Nextgov/FCW, which first reported the breach. DHS's Office of Intelligence and Analysis has conducted a damage assessment.

The intrusion comes as the US is overseeing security for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. A breach of the platform could expose security planning, interagency coordination, and response procedures for one of the most visible international events hosted in the country. HSIN is also used to exchange information about persons of interest and potential threats during emergencies. The hackers' affiliation and whether any documentation was exfiltrated remain unclear.

This is not HSIN's first security problem. In 2023, a contractor's coding error caused restricted data — including sensitive US person information — to be exposed to unauthorised users inside the platform, Wired previously reported. The full consequences of that earlier misconfiguration are still unclear. The incident also follows a suspected China-linked breach of an FBI surveillance system in February that likely exposed phone numbers of targets being monitored by the bureau, Nextgov/FCW reported.

DHS said in a statement: 'There is no indication that classified networks were impacted, and the system remains operational for our partners.'
sections.whyItMatters: A breach of DHS's primary information-sharing network — weeks before the World Cup and during a period of heightened national security concerns — exposes how fragile the unclassified coordination layer remains. The fact that attribution and scope are both still unknown makes this harder to assess than it would first appear, and the history of prior misconfiguration incidents raises questions about systemic weaknesses in the platform.

bottomLine: The central theme this week is the tension between control and access in AI technology. The US demonstrated it can shut down frontier models globally on a national security directive — and then reversed itself after 18 days of allied outcry. Simultaneously, it is holding back from blacklisting 100+ Chinese firms that its own security agencies have approved for listing, choosing diplomatic de-escalation over enforcement. CXMT's $3B Tencent deal shows what happens during the gap: domestic alternatives consolidate, and future export controls land on a target that has already moved out of range. The India-Japan economic security pact and the Kioxia/SK Hynix memory investments are the supply chain response — middle powers and allied chipmakers building alternatives to Chinese and American concentration alike. The question for the weeks ahead is whether the 'trusted partners' AI access concept at the G7 turns into a formal framework, or remains a dinner-table idea that died in Évian.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, Forbes, Nextgov/FCW, The Next Web, Global Times, ANI, CNBC TV18, Firstpost, SCMP, Nikkei Asia, Business Mirror, Memeburn, DigiTimes, China Daily, Barron's, IwaiCosmo Securities, Washington Post, Business Standard, Wired, HS Today
